topics.title: What is Python?
topics.content:
What is Python?

Python is a beginner-friendly, high-level programming language created by Guido van Rossum in 1991. It is one of the most popular programming languages in the world, used in web development, data science, artificial intelligence, and automation.

Why Python?

  • Clean, readable syntax — almost like English
  • Massive ecosystem of libraries (NumPy, Pandas, Django, etc.)
  • Used at Google, Instagram, NASA, and Netflix
  • Perfect as a first programming language
💡 Python uses indentation (4 spaces or 1 tab) to define code blocks — not curly braces like most languages.

topics.title: Your First Python Program
topics.content:
Hello, World!

By tradition, the first program you write in any language prints "Hello, World!". In Python it takes exactly one line:

print("Hello, World!")

Run it and you will see:

Hello, World!

How print() works

The print() function displays any value to the screen. You can print text (strings), numbers, or even the result of calculations:

print("Welcome to Python!")
print(42)
print(3 + 7)
💡 Strings (text) must be wrapped in quotes — either single ' or double ".

topics.title: Comments
topics.content:
Writing Comments

Comments are notes in your code that Python ignores when running. They help you and others understand what the code does.

# This is a single-line comment
print("Hello!")  # This comment is at the end of a line

"""
This is a multi-line comment
(actually a docstring, but commonly used as a comment)
"""

Why write comments?

  • Explain why you wrote the code a certain way
  • Leave notes for your future self
  • Temporarily disable code while testing
💡 Good programmers comment their code. Aim for comments that explain why, not what — the code itself shows what.

evalQuestions.question: Which function is used to display output in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: Think about the built-in function you use every time you want to show something on screen.

evalQuestions.question: How do you write a single-line comment in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: Python uses a symbol that looks like a hashtag.

evalQuestions.question: What will the following code print?
evalQuestions.hint: Evaluate the arithmetic expression inside print().

topics.title: Variables
topics.content:
Storing Data in Variables

A variable is a named container that stores data. In Python you create a variable simply by assigning a value — no keyword needed.

name = "Maria"   # text
age  = 20        # whole number
gpa  = 1.75      # decimal
is_enrolled = True  # yes/no

Naming Rules

  • Start with a letter or underscore (_)
  • Can contain letters, numbers, and underscores
  • Case-sensitive: Name ≠ name
  • Cannot use Python keywords (if, for, while, etc.)
💡 Python convention: use snake_case — lowercase words joined by underscores: student_name, total_score.

topics.title: Data Types
topics.content:
The Four Core Data Types

TypeExampleUse case
str"hello"Text and characters
int42Whole numbers
float3.14Decimal numbers
boolTrue / FalseLogic / yes-no values

Checking the Type

x = 42
print(type(x))      # <class 'int'>
print(type(3.14))   # <class 'float'>
print(type("hi"))   # <class 'str'>
print(type(True))   # <class 'bool'>

Type Conversion

print(int("42"))    # 42   str → int
print(float(5))     # 5.0  int → float
print(str(100))     # "100" int → str

topics.title: Operators
topics.content:
Arithmetic Operators

print(10 + 3)   # 13  — addition
print(10 - 3)   # 7   — subtraction
print(10 * 3)   # 30  — multiplication
print(10 / 3)   # 3.333… — division (always float)
print(10 // 3)  # 3   — floor division (drops decimal)
print(10 % 3)   # 1   — modulo (remainder)
print(2 ** 8)   # 256 — exponentiation

Comparison Operators

print(5 == 5)   # True
print(5 != 3)   # True
print(5 > 3)    # True
print(5 <= 3)   # False
⚠️ == checks equality; = assigns. Never confuse them!

evalQuestions.question: Which is the correct way to create a variable in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: Python does not require a keyword to declare a variable.

evalQuestions.question: What data type does Python assign to the value 3.14?
evalQuestions.hint: Numbers with decimal points are a specific numeric type.

evalQuestions.question: Fill in the blank to convert the string '42' to an integer.
evalQuestions.hint: Use the built-in function that converts to integer.

evalQuestions.question: What is the output of the following code?
evalQuestions.hint: % gives the remainder after division.

topics.title: if / elif / else
topics.content:
Making Decisions

The if statement runs code only when a condition is true.

grade = int(input("Enter grade: "))

if grade >= 90:
    print("Excellent!")
elif grade >= 75:
    print("Passed")
elif grade >= 60:
    print("Conditional Passed")
else:
    print("Failed")

Logical Operators

age = 20
is_enrolled = True

if age >= 18 and is_enrolled:
    print("Eligible")

if age < 18 or not is_enrolled:
    print("Not eligible")
💡 Python uses and, or, not — not &&, ||, !.

topics.title: for Loops
topics.content:
Repeating with for

A for loop iterates over a sequence — a range of numbers, a list, or any iterable.

# Loop 5 times
for i in range(5):
    print(i)        # prints 0 1 2 3 4

# Loop from 1 to 5
for i in range(1, 6):
    print(i)        # prints 1 2 3 4 5

# Loop over a list
fruits = ["apple", "mango", "banana"]
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

Loop Control

for i in range(10):
    if i == 5:
        break       # exit the loop
    if i % 2 == 0:
        continue    # skip even numbers
    print(i)        # prints 1 3

topics.title: while Loops
topics.content:
Repeating with while

A while loop keeps running as long as a condition is True.

count = 0
while count < 5:
    print("count:", count)
    count += 1      # Don't forget to update the condition!

# Output: count: 0  count: 1  count: 2  count: 3  count: 4

Infinite Loop Warning

# ⚠️ This runs forever — never do this!
# while True:
#     print("forever")
⚠️ Always make sure a while loop's condition will eventually become False, or use break to exit.

evalQuestions.question: Which is the correct syntax for an if statement in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: Python uses a colon, not brackets or 'then'.

evalQuestions.question: What does range(1, 5) produce?
evalQuestions.hint: range(start, stop) includes start but excludes stop.

evalQuestions.question: Arrange the lines to create a for loop that prints numbers 1 to 3.
evalQuestions.hint: The for statement comes first, then the print inside the loop.

topics.title: Defining Functions
topics.content:
Reusable Code Blocks

Functions let you write code once and call it many times.

def greet(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet("Maria")   # Hello, Maria!
greet("Juan")    # Hello, Juan!

Return Values

def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(3, 7)
print(result)   # 10

Default Parameters

def greet(name, greeting="Hello"):
    print(f"{greeting}, {name}!")

greet("Maria")           # Hello, Maria!
greet("Juan", "Kamusta") # Kamusta, Juan!
💡 Each function should do one thing. Keep them short and focused.

topics.title: Lists
topics.content:
Working with Lists

A list stores multiple values in an ordered, changeable sequence.

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

print(fruits[0])    # apple (first element)
print(fruits[-1])   # cherry (last element)
print(len(fruits))  # 3

# Modifying
fruits.append("mango")   # add to end
fruits.remove("banana")  # remove by value
fruits.pop()             # remove and return last
fruits.sort()            # sort alphabetically

List Slicing

nums = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(nums[1:4])    # [1, 2, 3]
print(nums[:3])     # [0, 1, 2]
print(nums[3:])     # [3, 4, 5]

topics.title: Input & Output
topics.content:
Getting User Input

name = input("Enter your name: ")
print(f"Hello, {name}!")
⚠️ input() always returns a string. Convert it if you need a number:
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))
height = float(input("Height in meters: "))

print(f"In 5 years you will be {age + 5}.")

f-Strings (Formatted Strings)

name = "Maria"
score = 95
print(f"Student: {name}, Score: {score}")
# Student: Maria, Score: 95

evalQuestions.question: What keyword is used to define a function in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: It is a short form of 'define'.

evalQuestions.question: Fill in the blank to add 'mango' to the end of the list.
evalQuestions.hint: Which list method adds an element to the end?

evalQuestions.question: Arrange the lines to define a function that returns the sum of two numbers, then call it.
evalQuestions.hint: Define the function before calling it.

evalQuestions.question: What is the output of the following code?
evalQuestions.hint: sum() adds all elements of a list.

topics.title: Dictionaries
topics.content:
Key-Value Storage

A dictionary stores data as key-value pairs. Use it when data has named attributes.

student = {
    "name": "Maria",
    "age": 20,
    "gpa": 1.75
}

print(student["name"])              # Maria
print(student.get("grade", "N/A")) # N/A (safe access)

student["year"] = 2    # add new key
del student["gpa"]     # remove key

# Iterating
for key, value in student.items():
    print(f"{key}: {value}")

topics.title: List Comprehensions
topics.content:
Elegant List Building

List comprehensions create lists in a single readable line.

# Traditional
squares = []
for x in range(5):
    squares.append(x**2)

# List comprehension (same result)
squares = [x**2 for x in range(5)]
print(squares)   # [0, 1, 4, 9, 16]

# With condition
evens = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
print(evens)     # [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

evalQuestions.question: What is a Python dictionary?
evalQuestions.hint: Think about how you look something up by name.

evalQuestions.question: What is the output of the following code?
evalQuestions.hint: dict.get(key, default) returns default if key is not found.

evalQuestions.question: What does [x**2 for x in range(5)] produce?
evalQuestions.hint: range(5) starts at 0.

topics.title: Classes and Objects
topics.content:
Object-Oriented Programming

OOP organises code around objects — bundles of data (attributes) and behaviour (methods).

class Student:
    school = "CodeArena"   # class attribute

    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name   # instance attribute
        self.age  = age

    def greet(self):
        print(f"Hi, I am {self.name}!")

s = Student("Maria", 20)
s.greet()   # Hi, I am Maria!

Inheritance

class GradStudent(Student):
    def __init__(self, name, age, thesis):
        super().__init__(name, age)
        self.thesis = thesis

topics.title: Error Handling
topics.content:
Handling Exceptions

try:
    age = int(input("Enter age: "))
    print(f"Next year: {age + 1}")
except ValueError:
    print("Please enter a valid number.")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Unexpected error: {e}")
else:
    print("No errors occurred!")
finally:
    print("This always runs.")

Common Exceptions

  • ValueError — invalid value (e.g. int("abc"))
  • TypeError — wrong type
  • IndexError — list index out of range
  • KeyError — missing dictionary key
  • ZeroDivisionError — dividing by zero

evalQuestions.question: What does the __init__ method do in a class?
options: Initialises instance attributes when an object is created
evalQuestions.hint: It is called automatically when you create a new object.

evalQuestions.question: Which exception is raised when dividing by zero?
evalQuestions.hint: The exception name describes exactly what happened.

evalQuestions.question: Fill in the correct exception type for invalid integer conversion.
evalQuestions.hint: int('abc') raises a specific exception related to the value.

evalQuestions.question: Arrange the lines to define a Person class and call its greet method.
evalQuestions.hint: Define the class and its methods before creating an instance.

topics.title: Generators
topics.content:
Memory-Efficient Sequences

Generators produce values one at a time using yield, without building a full list in memory.

def countdown(n):
    while n > 0:
        yield n
        n -= 1

for val in countdown(3):
    print(val)   # 3, 2, 1

Generator Expressions

# List (all in memory)
squares_list = [x**2 for x in range(1000000)]

# Generator (lazy, efficient)
squares_gen = (x**2 for x in range(1000000))
💡 Use generators when you work with large data sets or infinite sequences — they are far more memory-efficient than lists.

topics.title: Decorators
topics.content:
Wrapping Functions

A decorator wraps a function to add behaviour without modifying its code.

def my_decorator(func):
    def wrapper():
        print("Before")
        func()
        print("After")
    return wrapper

@my_decorator
def say_hello():
    print("Hello!")

say_hello()
# Before
# Hello!
# After

Practical: Timing Decorator

import time

def timer(func):
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        start = time.time()
        result = func(*args, **kwargs)
        print(f"Elapsed: {time.time()-start:.4f}s")
        return result
    return wrapper

evalQuestions.question: What makes a function a generator in Python?
evalQuestions.hint: Generators produce values one at a time.

evalQuestions.question: What is the output of the following code?
evalQuestions.hint: Trace what the decorator wraps around the original call.

evalQuestions.question: Fill in the blank to complete the generator.
evalQuestions.hint: What keyword makes a function a generator?

topics.title: Introduction to JavaScript
topics.content:
What is JavaScript?

JavaScript is the programming language of the web. It runs in every browser and lets you make pages interactive — responding to clicks, fetching data, and updating content without a page reload.

Where does JavaScript run?

  • Browser — client-side interactivity (animations, forms, games)
  • Server — Node.js powers backends like Discord and Netflix
  • Mobile — React Native apps

Hello, World!

console.log("Hello, World!");

topics.title: Variables and Data Types
topics.content:
Storing Data

let name    = "Maria";    // string
let age     = 20;          // number
let gpa     = 1.75;        // number (float)
let enrolled = true;       // boolean
const PI    = 3.14159;     // constant — cannot reassign

var vs let vs const

KeywordScopeReassignable
varfunction✓
letblock✓
constblock✗
💡 Prefer const by default; use let when you need to reassign. Avoid var.

topics.title: Functions & Arrays
topics.content:
Functions

// Function declaration
function greet(name) {
  return `Hello, ${name}!`;
}

// Arrow function (modern)
const square = n => n * n;

console.log(greet("Maria"));  // Hello, Maria!
console.log(square(5));        // 25

Arrays

const fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"];
console.log(fruits[0]);           // apple
console.log(fruits.length);       // 3

fruits.push("mango");             // add to end
fruits.pop();                     // remove last

const evens = [1,2,3,4].filter(n => n % 2 === 0);
console.log(evens);               // [2, 4]

evalQuestions.question: Which keyword declares a block-scoped variable in modern JavaScript?
evalQuestions.hint: Introduced in ES6, it replaced var for most use cases.

evalQuestions.question: What does === check in JavaScript?
evalQuestions.hint: It is stricter than ==.

evalQuestions.question: What is the output of the following code?
evalQuestions.hint: += adds to the current value.

evalQuestions.question: Arrange the lines to define and call a function that squares a number.
evalQuestions.hint: Declare the function before calling it.
